nameMeanings.name: Bucknell
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of the places called Bucknell in Oxfordshire and Shropshire, Bucknall in Lincolnshire and Somerset, or Bucknowl in Dorset. These are all named with the Old English byname Bucca (see Buck ) or Old English bucca ‘he-goat’ (with genitive -n) + hyll ‘hill’ in the first two examples or healh ‘nook, hollow’ in the latter two. Bucknowl in Dorset, recorded as Bubecnolle in 1285, is named from the Old English personal name Bubba (see Bubb ) + cnoll ‘hill top’.

variants.name: Buck
meanings: English: nickname from Middle English buc(ke) ‘male goat’ (Old English bucca) or a ‘male deer’ (Old English bucc). The goat was popularly associated with lecherous behaviour and the deer with timidity and speed. The surname may also be a shortened form of longer occupational names, for example Roger le Bucmanger' ‘dealer in bucks or venison’, (Warwickshire 1221) or Walter Bucswayn perhaps ‘goatherd’ (Somerset 1327).
English: topographic name for someone who lived near a prominent beech tree, such as Peter atte Buk (Suffolk 1327), from Middle English buk ‘beech’ (from Old English bōc).
German and Dutch (Limburg): from a personal name, a short form of Burkhard (see Burkhart ).

nameMeanings.name: Thorold
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: relationship name from the Anglo-Norman and Middle English personal name T(h)urold, T(h)orold, T(h)orald (Old Scandinavian Þóraldr). The initial T is due to the Anglo-Norman pronunciation of Th-. Torald and Torold became Toraud and Toroud, giving the modern surname Torode. This name may have become confused with Tyrrell , Turrill , and Torrell .

variants.name: Tyrrell
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, which is common in Ireland, but of uncertain derivation. It may have originated as a nickname for a stubborn person, from Old French tirel, used of an animal which pulls on the reins, a derivative of tirer ‘to pull’.

variants.name: Turrell
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, a variant of   Tyrell .

variants.name: Torrell
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, a variant of   Tyrell .

historicEvents.title: Vikings Invade England
historicEvents.description: Viking raiders from Scandinavia invaded England in 980. Years later in 991, the English government paid them 10,000 in hopes for peace. The Viking raiders, however, continued to force more money from England.

historicEvents.title: Danes Attack London
historicEvents.description: Danes and Norwegians attacked London unsuccessfully in 994.

historicEvents.title: St. Brice's Day Massacre
historicEvents.description: King Aethelred ordered all Danish men in England to be killed. It is unknown how many Danes were killed due to this order.

historicEvents.title: Sweyn Forkbeard
historicEvents.description: The Danes raided and captured England. Sweyn, King of Danes, was declared King of England on Christmas Day.

historicEvents.title: Canut the Great
historicEvents.description: As Aethelred, King of England died, his son, Edmund Ironside devised a truce with Danish King Canute. The truce divided the kingdom between the two but Edmund died shortly after and became King of England. Several years later, he also became king of Denmark.

historicEvents.title: England Divided
historicEvents.description: King Canute died in 1035 and England was divided between his three sons. His son Sweyn received Norway, Harthacanute received Denmark, and Harold Harefoot received North England.

en.lifeSummary: Thorold Bucknell of Coventry, Buckingham, Sheriff of Lincolnshire was born about 0955, in Lincolnshire, England as the son of Godevin de Bukenhall I. He had at least 2 daughters with Elfwined Malet Of Lincoln. He died on 31 August 1057, in King's Bromley, Staffordshire, England, at the age of 103.
